elements.sent-2411-054: Given a desired composition, a synthesis technique should ideally be capable of mixing metals in nanoparticles with controlled size and mass loading.

elements.sent-2411-056: Another challenge in synthesis is that the alloys need to be deposited on a carbon gas diffusion layer (GDL) to facilitate gas reactions for catalyst testing.

elements.sent-2411-059: We use both of these approaches to synthesize binary and ternary metal alloys in our study since they have complementary elemental accessibility, see Figure \ref{fig:chemical_space .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Duplicate samples were synthesized in a subset of the experiments to aid in verifying the reproducibility of the sample synthesis and testing procedures.
elements.sent-2411-061: Spark ablation is a dry method, which uses additive printing with electrical energy to fragment metal rods into nanoclusters under an inert gas that can be directly deposited on a GDL.
elements.sent-2411-062: This was accomplished using a VSParticle© nanoparticle generator (VSP-P1).
elements.sent-2411-063: The generator relies on a physical phenomenon similar to vaporization and condensation, which uses high-voltage sparks between two closely spaced metal or alloy rods to generate a localized plasma.
elements.sent-2411-064: The intense heat from the plasma vaporizes material from these rods (ablation), which then cools and condenses into nanoparticles.
elements.sent-2411-065: Using an impaction technique and a 3D printer, nanoparticles can be synthesized and printed in a few hours.
elements.sent-2411-066: After printing a sample on the GDL, it is annealed using a tube furnace at 400\degree C temperatures with reducing gas to target intermetallic alloyed nanoparticles.
elements.sent-2411-067: See Section \ref{sec:SI_sparksynth for details on this process.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Chemical reduction was performed using an automated robotic system (Chemspeed) that performs wet chemistry operations.
elements.sent-2411-069: This approach uses metal salt precursors that are mixed and reduced using a reducing agent that donates electrons to the mixed metals.
elements.sent-2411-070: The resulting sample is then post-annealed at elevated temperatures to create alloyed nanoparticle powders.

elements.sent-2411-096: This tool compares experimental \gls{XRD pattern, i.e., peak position and intensity array, with computational \gls{XRD patterns that are generated from structures in the Materials Project, OQMD, and Alexandria, as well as experimental references from the Crystallography Open Database (COD) \cite{Grazulis2012 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The process is conducted in four steps:.
elements.sent-2411-098: Similarity Analysis (Figure \ref{fig:xrd_step1 ): We start by matching the position and intensity of experimental \gls{XRD pattern with simulated patterns from computational structures.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Multiphase matching (Figure \ref{fig:xrd_step2 ): Once a phase is matched, we remove it from the experimental \gls{XRD pattern.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: We then repeat the first step to find additional phases.
elements.sent-2411-101: This process continues until all phases are identified or a user-defined limit is reached.
elements.sent-2411-102: Matching goodness evaluation (Figure \ref{fig:xrd_step3 ): Finally, we refine the XRD fit using Rietveld refinement\cite{mccusker1999rietveld, baptista2022xerus .}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This step checks how well the identified phases match the experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-104: A quick and rough Rietveld fitting helps us assess the quality of the match by calculating the weighted profile R score (R$_{wp}$), which evaluates the fit’s quality (the lower, the better).
elements.sent-2411-105: We report fits that are below an $R_{wp}$ threshold of 50, as larger values typically indicate poor-quality fits that do not provide additional value to our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-106: To find the best crystal structure match to the synthesized sample, we look for XRD matching solutions (phase mixtures) containing one phase that meet the following two conditions:.
elements.sent-2411-107: The major phase has a weight fraction more than 70 wt.\% of the phase mixture.
elements.sent-2411-108: The composition (atomic fraction) in the matched phase is close to what we measured in the sample using \gls{XRF (within $\pm$ two standard deviations).}
elements.sent-2411-109: We determine the standard deviation by measuring the \gls{XRF composition at three different spots on the sample.}
elements.sent-2411-110: We then rank the phases that meet the above criteria based on the refinement $R_{wp}$ score.
elements.sent-2411-111: We consider the phase to be a potential match using a score we call $q$, which is calculated as follows: \begin{equation}q_i = 100 \cdot \exp(-\alpha \cdot (R_{wp,i} - R_{wp,min}))\end{equation} In this equation, $\alpha$ is a constant scaling factor set to 0.05.
elements.sent-2411-112: The $q$-score is used to assess the goodness of fit relative to other possible solutions.
elements.sent-2411-113: In this work, we designate samples as matched if they have a phase with weight fraction $\geq$70 wt.\%, $q_{i \geq 70$ and an R$_{wp \leq 40$.}}

elements.sent-2411-142: Catalyst performance for nanoparticles is an aggregate property over many surfaces, which may or may not be catalytically active.
elements.sent-2411-143: A predictive model may combine surface-level descriptors (e.g., adsorption energies) with bulk material properties (e.g., Matminer features) to estimate the experimental performance of the aggregated material.
elements.sent-2411-144: It is clear how material-level properties can be leveraged to predict other material-level properties, but aggregating surface-level properties to predict material-level properties is more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-145: This is in part due to the fact that the estimation of which surfaces will be realized experimentally is a difficult \cite{tao2011situ, zafeiratos2012alloys and open problem.}
elements.sent-2411-146: We explore three approaches to combining surface-level descriptors.
elements.sent-2411-147: First, we consider the simplest case - using the mean adsorption energy across all surfaces: \begin{equation}E_{ads,mean} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i^N E_{ads,i}\end{equation} where $i$ iterates over the $N$ surfaces and $E_{ads,i}$ is the surface's adsorption energy.
elements.sent-2411-148: The second uses Boltzmann weighting for both the adsorption energies and the surface energies given the set of surfaces using: \begin{equation}E_{ads,Boltz.} = \frac{\sum_i^N E_{ads,i} e^{-(E_{ads,i} - E_{ads,min})/(k_b T)} e^{-(E_{cl,i} - E_{cl,min})/(k_b T)}}{\sum_i^N e^{-(E_{ads,i} - E_{ads,min})/(k_b T)} e^{-(E_{cl,i} - E_{cl,min})/(k_b T)}}\end{equation} where $E_{cl,i}$ is the cleavage energy on that surface and $E_{ads,min}$ is the material's minimum adsorption energy and similarly for $E_{cl,min}$.

elements.sent-2411-151: The third approach uses the Wulff construction\cite{ringe2011wulff , which is a minimization of the total energy over a nanoparticle as a function of the surface energies of the various possible terminations with different geometric character\cite{dobrushin1992wulff .}}
elements.sent-2411-152: For non-unary materials, it can be difficult to calculate surface energies, but the cleavage energy may be used as a proxy.

elements.sent-2411-325: In this work, the VSP-P1 Nanoprinter was used to synthesize inorganic nanoporous thin films with different binary and ternary elemental composition.
elements.sent-2411-326: The P1 uses three sequential automated steps to convert a solid rod into particles smaller than 5 nm and print them in the form of nanoporous layers.
elements.sent-2411-327: In the first step, two solid rods are placed with the tips closely spaced to each other.
elements.sent-2411-328: Electrical sparks are formed between the rods to create a local plasma.
elements.sent-2411-329: Due to the high temperature of the plasma (>20,000$\degree$C), small parts of the rods are evaporated into an atomic cluster.
elements.sent-2411-330: Directly after the spark, the loose atoms bump into each other and grow into bigger clusters/nanoparticles.
elements.sent-2411-331: In the second step, the evaporated material is transported by a carrier gas that flows in between the electrodes.
elements.sent-2411-332: Since the carrier gas is at room temperature, it accelerates the cooling of the hot gas after the plasma stage.
elements.sent-2411-333: Due to this fast quenching, the particles of the cluster stabilize (e.g. stop aggregating) at 2 – 5 nanometers in size.
elements.sent-2411-334: During their time-of-flight, these stable primary nanoparticles could still form agglomerates by sticking to and colliding with each other.
elements.sent-2411-335: In the final step, the impaction method is used to get the growing particles out of the carrier gas and printed on a substrate.
elements.sent-2411-336: This is achieved by accelerating the gas flow to supersonic speeds by forcing them through a nozzle with a narrow orifice.
elements.sent-2411-337: At this speed, the growing nanoparticles are not able to follow the gas flow but instead follow a straight trajectory and immobilize on the substrate.
elements.sent-2411-338: In this way, thin-film nanoporous layers of nanoparticles can be formed with different thicknesses.
elements.sent-2411-339: Since the substrate is mounted on an XYZ-gantry stage, the nanoparticles can be printed in all kind of complex patterns.
elements.sent-2411-340: It is important to mention that because of the small size of the nanoparticles, they have a very strong Van der Waals force which make them stick to any surface or other nanoparticles.
elements.sent-2411-341: This unique feature enables the VSP-P1 to print sticking layers on almost any substrate without adding binders.
elements.sent-2411-342: A dedicated P1 was used for this project with the following specs:.
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Calibrating the printer for different materials
elements.sent-2411-350: Due to differences in evaporation enthalpy, it is important to calibrate the amount of material that is deposited on the substrate.
elements.sent-2411-351: This calibration is specific for element type, electrode diameter, carrier gas type, flow, nozzle size, and distance between nozzle and substrate.
elements.sent-2411-352: Calibration is performed by printing on carbon paper \gls{GDL (type H23C3 from fuelcellstore.com) for different periods of time and measuring the mass increase.}
elements.sent-2411-353: Plotting these points on a graph with an intercept of zero gives a slope that represents the printing rate in milligrams per hour.
elements.sent-2411-354: The calibration is done with two of the VSP-G1 sources connected, including multiple times with cleaning of the G1 in between prints.
elements.sent-2411-355: Calibration was performed under fixed operating conditions: the gas flow rate in both G1s were 1.35 L/min Ar/H2 (95/5), and the spark settings were 1.3 kV and 10 mA with a spark frequency of 220 Hz.
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Controlling oxidation of the samples during shipment for electrochemical testing
elements.sent-2411-356: Since most of the elemental analysis was conducted at the University of Toronto's facilities, we investigated the impact of shipping on the samples.
elements.sent-2411-357: The nanoporous metallic samples were expected to oxidize when exposed to air (oxygen).
elements.sent-2411-358: However, we discovered that packaging the samples under inert gases like Argon still resulted in oxidation, despite using cycles of vacuuming and purging with inert gas.
elements.sent-2411-359: Surprisingly, packaging the samples in air did not lead to oxidation.
elements.sent-2411-360: XRD analysis revealed that the samples remained metallic for more than six days when exposed to air.
elements.sent-2411-361: Annealing the samples under a gas mixture of 95\% Ar and 5\% $H_2$ at 400$\degree$C for 30 minutes after shipment was found to reduce surface oxides and alloy the metal mixtures\cite{dai2023sub .}
elements.sent-2411-362: During the annealing process, the particle size is increased gradually until a layer is formed.
elements.sent-2411-363: By optimizing the annealing temperature the alloy can be formed without losing to much of the porosity.
elements.sent-2411-364: Annealing was limited to relatively low temperatures and for short times to prevent nanoparticle growth and reactivity reduction.
elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Controlling the elemental composition by mixing 2 or 3 sources
elements.sent-2411-365: A standard VSP-P1 is able to work with 2 VSP-G1 nanoparticle sources.
elements.sent-2411-366: The output of the 2 sources is combined into a single gas stream that is entering the printer nozzle.
elements.sent-2411-367: If one sources is setup with gold electrodes and the other with copper electrodes the P1 is printing nanoporous layers made of stacked gold and copper nanoparticles.
elements.sent-2411-368: For this study, VSP modified the P1 to accommodate a 3rd VSP-G1 source to make ternary compositions as well as binary.
elements.sent-2411-369: The composition of the particles in the gas stream can be tuned by lowering the power settings for one, or two in the case where three sources are used, of the G1s.
elements.sent-2411-370: One G1 is kept at 1.3 kV and 10 mA as max settings.
elements.sent-2411-371: Lowering the output is done by lowering the current and keep the voltage constant.
elements.sent-2411-372: In this way the gap between the electrodes stays constant and only lowers the frequency of sparking.
elements.sent-2411-373: The frequency cannot be set in the G1, but rather it is a result of the combination between voltage and current that is chosen.
elements.sent-2411-374: There is a limit to this, however, if the frequency drops below around 20 Hz the sparking becomes unstable.
elements.sent-2411-375: This happens when the current is below 1.1 mA and the voltage has been lowered to 0.5 kV.
elements.sent-2411-376: The loading/layer thickness is tuned by the movement speed of the stage during printing.
elements.subsubsection-2411-005: Printing the same elemental composition on wafer and GDE substrates
elements.sent-2411-377: The initial goal of the synthesis was to deposit sample on a silicon wafer to enable characterization by XRF and XRD without peak interference in the region of interest.
elements.sent-2411-378: This involved printing 2x2 mm squares of various compositions on the wafer, achieving a loading of 0.2 $mg/cm^{2}$ by stacking five layers sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-380: After annealing the Si wafer, XRD was used to identify the crystal structure of the samples, which were then replicated on \gls{GDE for performance testing.}
elements.sent-2411-381: However, XRF revealed source output deviations of 1-15\% from the setpoint, complicating the replication of exact compositions on GDEs.
elements.sent-2411-382: To resolve this, we began printing wafer and GDE samples simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-383: Each batch included a 4-inch wafer and a 10x10 cm GDE substrate, allowing consistent and stable G1 settings.
elements.sent-2411-384: Layers were alternated between the wafer and GDE to average out compositional drift.
elements.sent-2411-385: The automated synthesis was performed using the Chemspeed Swing XL platform (Figures \ref{fig:chemspeed_layout and \ref{fig:chemspeed_photo ).}}
elements.sent-2411-386: Metal precursor stock solutions (0.15 M) and a hydrazine/NaOH(2.67 M) reducing solution (25:27, v/v) were manually prepared and loaded into the platform.
elements.sent-2411-387: Precise ratios of these stock solutions were mixed to achieve the desired compositions (1.5 mL total per tube).
elements.sent-2411-388: After thorough mixing, 0.65 mL of hydrazine/NaOH solution were added and ultrasonicated at 50$\degree$C for one hour.
elements.sent-2411-389: The process concluded with three purification cycles involving ultrasonication and \gls{DI water rinses and, lastly, the solutions were dried in a vacuum oven (Figure \ref{fig:chemical_reduction_workflow ).}}

elements.sent-2411-413: We considered all materials available in three permissively-licensed computational materials databases: \gls{MP ~\cite{MP_main , \gls{OQMD ~\cite{oqmd1, oqmd2 , and Alexandria~\cite{dcgat, cgat .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-414: In an effort to improve the likelihood of discovering an industrially viable catalyst, materials were filtered by assessing their thermodynamic stability under reaction conditions.
elements.sent-2411-415: To do this, we evaluated the decomposition energy using the Pourbaix framework implemented in pymatgen\cite{pbx_efficient, pymatgen, pbx_main .}
elements.sent-2411-416: We were interested in neutral pH conditions (in the bulk), but the pH will be elevated at the electrode under operating conditions\cite{billy2017experimental, zhang2020direct .}
elements.sent-2411-417: Materials with a decomposition energy less than 0.05 eV/atom anywhere in the operating range of pH (7 to 14) and applied potential (-1.35 to -0.55 V versus SHE) were retained.
elements.sent-2411-418: To facilitate this analysis despite any small differences in \gls{DFT settings, we determined a subset of materials within the non-MP databases with analogous entries in MP.}
elements.sent-2411-419: Using the formation energies of these materials in each of the databases, we fit linear corrections to the formation energies to be MP-like (see Figure \ref{fig:form_e_corrections ).}
elements.sent-2411-420: From the Pourbaix analysis, we found 2,458 materials from \gls{MP , 3,173 materials from \gls{OQMD , and 13,775 materials from Alexandria for use in our computational pipeline.}}
elements.sent-2411-421: The bulk materials were then re-optimized with \gls{RPBE to be consistent with the level of theory of \gls{OC20 , which the \gls{ML models used in this work were trained on.}}}

elements.sent-2411-426: Catalyst slabs were created from relaxed bulks using pymatgen\cite{pymatgen implemented code to enumerate all surfaces up to Miller index 2.}

elements.sent-2411-429: All surface and placement enumeration code is provided at https://github.com/Open-Catalyst-Project/Open-Catalyst-Dataset.

elements.sent-2411-432: A total of $\sim700,000$ unique surfaces were generated across the selected materials.
elements.sent-2411-433: Placements were made with several modes: full heuristic placements, random sites with heuristic placements, and full random placements.
elements.sent-2411-434: For random sites, a Delaunay meshgrid is constructed with sites uniformly sampled across the grid.
elements.sent-2411-435: For heuristic, sites are assigned at each surface atom (atop), between every pair of adjacent surface atoms (bridge), and at the center of every group of three or four adjacent surface atoms (hollow).
elements.sent-2411-436: For all approaches, the adsorbate binding atom is placed onto the site, then the adsorbate is translated along the surface normal until the minimum distance between any adsorbate atom and any surface atom is 0.1 \AA{ .}
elements.sent-2411-437: Additionally, to increase the diversity of initial configurations considered, the adsorbate is uniformly randomly rotated around the z-direction and provided a slight wobble around x and y, which amounts to a randomized tilt within a certain cone around the north pole.
elements.sent-2411-438: For even greater diversity, random rotations in all directions about the center of mass are applied for the fully random placement mode.
elements.sent-2411-439: Placements made before updates followed a similar strategy but used CatKit\cite{catkit for heuristic placements and restricted random rotations to only the z-axis.}
elements.sent-2411-440: These changes were shown to have negligible effect on the performance of adsorption energy calculations, as shown by Lan et al.\cite{lan2023adsorbml .}
elements.sent-2411-441: Screening the proposed search space for \gls{CO2RR with \gls{DFT would be an intractable problem for even the world's most powerful supercomputers.}}
elements.sent-2411-442: To address this, recent work \cite{chanussot2021open, lan2023adsorbml has demonstrated the utility of generalized \gls{ML potentials for catalyst discovery.}}
elements.sent-2411-443: The incredible progress the community has made in the development of more accurate models has made the reality of large screening campaigns like this possible.
elements.sent-2411-444: For all adsorbate and surface combinations, we calculate the adsorption energies using an AdsorbML\cite{lan2023adsorbml ML+SP approach.}
elements.sent-2411-445: Given an adsorbate-surface combination, we first enumerate $\sim$165 different adsorbate configurations (100 random + $\sim$65 heuristic) on the surface.
elements.sent-2411-446: Unlike a \gls{DFT relaxation that can take roughly 24 hours per relaxation, \gls{ML models used in this work finish in as little as five seconds.}}
elements.sent-2411-447: Once the \gls{ML relaxed states are computed, the five lowest-energy systems are selected and a single point \gls{DFT call is made on the \gls{ML relaxed structure to get a more accurate adsorption energy.}}}

elements.sent-2411-450: All single-point calculations were performed with a maximum number of electronic steps of 300 to ensure that the initialized wavefunction had sufficient steps to converge.
